q: What happens to the inductive reactance and the current in a purely inductive circuit if the frequency is halved ?
question: What happens to the inductive reactance and the current in a purely inductive circuit if the frequency is halved ?
options:
Both, inductive reactance and current will
be halved.

Inductive reactance will be halved and
current will be doubled..

Inductive reactance will be doubled and
current will be halved.
Both, inducting reactance and current will be doubled
solution:
XL=ωL

i=v0ωL

q: A power transmission line feeds input power at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kV</mi></math> to a step down transformer with its primary winding having <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3000</mn><mo> </mo><mtext>turns</mtext></math>. The output power is delivered at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>230</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> by the transformer. The current in the primary of the transformer is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and its efficiency is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>90</mn><mo>%</mo></math>. The winding of transformer is made of copper. The output current of transformer is ____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>.
question: A power transmission line feeds input power at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kV</mi></math> to a step down transformer with its primary winding having <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3000</mn><mo> </mo><mtext>turns</mtext></math>. The output power is delivered at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>230</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> by the transformer. The current in the primary of the transformer is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and its efficiency is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>90</mn><mo>%</mo></math>. The winding of transformer is made of copper. The output current of transformer is ____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>.
solution:
Given:

Input power, Pi=2300×5 watt

Output power, P0=2300×5×0.9

Now, we can write η=PoutPin=VSISVPIP
⇒0.9=230 IS2300×5⇒IS=45 A.

subject: Physics
q: The figure given below shows an $LCR$ series circuit with two switches $S_1$ and $S_2$. When switch $S_1$ is closed keeping $S_2$ open, the phase difference $(\phi)$ between the current and source voltage is $30°$ and phase difference is $60°$ when $S_2$ is closed keeping $S_1$ open. The value of $(3L_1 - L_2)$ is _______ H.
question: The figure given below shows an $LCR$ series circuit with two switches $S_1$ and $S_2$. When switch $S_1$ is closed keeping $S_2$ open, the phase difference $(\phi)$ between the current and source voltage is $30°$ and phase difference is $60°$ when $S_2$ is closed keeping $S_1$ open. The value of $(3L_1 - L_2)$ is _______ H.
solution:
From the source $v = V_0 \sin(300t)$, the angular frequency is $\omega = 300\text{ rad/s}$.
Capacitance $C = 100\ \mu\text{F} = 10^{-4}\text{ F}$, so $X_C = \dfrac{1}{\omega C}$.

Case 1 ($S_1$ closed, $S_2$ open):
Closing $S_1$ short-circuits $L_2$, leaving an LCR series circuit with $C$, $R$ and $L_1$. With phase difference $\phi_1 = 30^\circ$:
$\tan 30^\circ = \dfrac{\omega L_1 - X_C}{R}$
$\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} = \dfrac{\omega L_1 - X_C}{R} \Rightarrow R = \sqrt{3}\,(\omega L_1 - X_C)$ ... (1)

Case 2 ($S_2$ closed, $S_1$ open):
Closing $S_2$ short-circuits $L_1$, leaving an LCR series circuit with $C$, $R$ and $L_2$. With phase difference $\phi_2 = 60^\circ$:
$\tan 60^\circ = \dfrac{\omega L_2 - X_C}{R}$
$\sqrt{3} = \dfrac{\omega L_2 - X_C}{R} \Rightarrow \omega L_2 - X_C = R\sqrt{3}$ ... (2)

Substituting $R$ from (1) into (2):
$\omega L_2 - X_C = \sqrt{3}\,(\omega L_1 - X_C) \cdot \sqrt{3}$
$\omega L_2 - X_C = 3\omega L_1 - 3X_C$
$2X_C = 3\omega L_1 - \omega L_2 = \omega(3L_1 - L_2)$

Therefore:
$3L_1 - L_2 = \dfrac{2X_C}{\omega} = \dfrac{2}{\omega^2 C}$

Substituting $\omega = 300\text{ rad/s}$ and $C = 10^{-4}\text{ F}$:
$\omega^2 C = (300)^2 \cdot 10^{-4} = 9 \times 10^4 \cdot 10^{-4} = 9$

$3L_1 - L_2 = \dfrac{2}{9}\text{ H}$

Hence, the correct option is $(2)\ \dfrac{2}{9}$.

q: A series LCR circuit is designed to resonate at an angular frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>ω</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>5</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>rad</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The circuit draws <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi></math> power from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>120</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> source at resonance. The value of resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> in the circuit is ________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi><mo>.</mo></math>

The value of resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> in the circuit is ________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
At resonance

P=V2R

R=V2P=120216

=900 Ω

q: For an RLC circuit driven with voltage of amplitude <math><msub><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>m</mi></mrow></msub></math> and frequency <math><msub><mrow><mtext>ω</mtext></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><msqrt><mi>L</mi><mi>C</mi></msqrt></mrow></mfrac></math> the current exhibits resonance. The quality factor, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> is given by
question: For an RLC circuit driven with voltage of amplitude <math><msub><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>m</mi></mrow></msub></math> and frequency <math><msub><mrow><mtext>ω</mtext></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><msqrt><mi>L</mi><mi>C</mi></msqrt></mrow></mfrac></math> the current exhibits resonance. The quality factor, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> is given by
solution:
The quality factor of an L.C.R. circuit is given by, 

(Quality factor) Q=1RLC...(i)

Where L,R,C are the inductance, resistance, and conductance of the L.C.R. circuit. The given resonance frequency is, ω0=1LC

⇒C=1ω0L  ...(ii)

From (i) and (ii)

⇒Q=1RL×ω0L⇒Q=ω0LR

q: A sinusoidal voltage of peak value <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>250</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> is applied to a series <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi><mi>C</mi><mi>R</mi></math> circuit, in which <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>L</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>24</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mH</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></math>. The value of power dissipated at resonant condition is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>kW</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> to the nearest integer is _______.
question: A sinusoidal voltage of peak value <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>250</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> is applied to a series <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi><mi>C</mi><mi>R</mi></math> circuit, in which <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>L</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>24</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mH</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></math>. The value of power dissipated at resonant condition is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>kW</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> to the nearest integer is _______.
solution:
At resonance power, P=Vrms2R

P=250/228=3906.25 W

≈4 kW

subject: Physics
q: If wattless current flows in the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>AC</mi></math> circuit, then the circuit is :
question: If wattless current flows in the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>AC</mi></math> circuit, then the circuit is :
solution: The current in an AC circuit containing only either a capacitor or an inductor is said to be wattless as the average power is zero. As wattless current has phase difference of ϕ=90° with voltage, therefore power factor cosϕ will be zero.

q: A series combination of resistor of resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> inductor of inductance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math> and capacitor of capacitance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μF</mi></math> is connected to an ac source. The quality factor of the circuit will be _____.
question: A series combination of resistor of resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> inductor of inductance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math> and capacitor of capacitance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μF</mi></math> is connected to an ac source. The quality factor of the circuit will be _____.
solution:
The formula to calculate the quality factor of an LCR circuit is given by

Q=1RLC   ...1

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (1) to calculate the required quality factor of the circuit.

Q=110016.25×10-6=1100×10002.5=4

subject: Physics
q: A sinusoidal voltage <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi><mfenced><mi>t</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>210</mn><mi>sin</mi><mn>3000</mn><mi>t</mi></math> volt is applied to a series LCR circuit in which <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mH</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>C</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μF</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>100</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>. The phase difference <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>Φ</mi></mfenced></math> between the applied voltage and resultant current will be
question: A sinusoidal voltage <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi><mfenced><mi>t</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>210</mn><mi>sin</mi><mn>3000</mn><mi>t</mi></math> volt is applied to a series LCR circuit in which <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mH</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>C</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μF</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>100</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>. The phase difference <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>Φ</mi></mfenced></math> between the applied voltage and resultant current will be
solution:
Capacitive reactance is XC=1ωC=13000×25×10-6=403Ω

Inductive reactance is XL=ωL=3000×10×10-3=30Ω

Now, total reactance is X=XL-XC

=30-403=503Ω

tanϕ=XR=503100=16

tanϕ=16=0.17

Hence, the phase difference between the applied voltage and resultant current is tan-10.17.

The quality factor of the circuit is :
solution: The quality factor of LCR circuit is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>ω</mi><mi>L</mi></mrow><mi>R</mi></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>R</mi></mfrac><msqrt><mfrac><mi>L</mi><mi>C</mi></mfrac></msqrt><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>100</mn></mfrac><msqrt><mfrac><mrow><mn>80</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfrac></msqrt><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn></math>

q: A sinusoidal voltage of peak value <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>283</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> and angular frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>320</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is applied to a series <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi><mi>C</mi><mi>R</mi></math> circuit. Given that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>5 </mn><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mi>L</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>25</mn><mi> </mi><mi>mH</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1000</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μF</mi></math>.<mo></mo><mn></mn><mi></mi><mi></mi> The total impedance and phase difference between the voltage across the source and the current will respectively be-
question: A sinusoidal voltage of peak value <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>283</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> and angular frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>320</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is applied to a series <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi><mi>C</mi><mi>R</mi></math> circuit. Given that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>5 </mn><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mi>L</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>25</mn><mi> </mi><mi>mH</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1000</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μF</mi></math>.<mo></mo><mn></mn><mi></mi><mi></mi> The total impedance and phase difference between the voltage across the source and the current will respectively be-
solution:
ε0=283 volt,  ω=320 s-1

XL=320×25×10-3=8 Ω

XC=1ωC=1320×1000×10-6

XC=10000320=3.1 Ω

R=5Ω

Z= R2+XL-XC2= 50 ≈ 7Ω

tan⁡ϕ=XL-XCR=1

⟹ϕ=45o

subject: Physics
q: In the given figure, the charge stored in 6μF capacitor, when points A and B are joined by a connecting wire is _______μC.
question: In the given figure, the charge stored in 6μF capacitor, when points A and B are joined by a connecting wire is _______μC.
solution:
At steady state, capacitor behaves as an open circuit and current flows in circuit as shown in the diagram.

Req=9 Ω

i=9 V9Ω=1 A

ΔV6Ω=1×6=6 V

VA=3 V

So, potential difference across 6μF is 6 V.

Hence, Q=CΔV

=6×6×10-6 C

=36 μC

subject: Physics
q: In a scries <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi><mi>C</mi><mi>R</mi></math> resonance circuit, if we change the resistance only, from a lower to higher value :
question: In a scries <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi><mi>C</mi><mi>R</mi></math> resonance circuit, if we change the resistance only, from a lower to higher value :
options: The bandwidth of resonance circuit will increase.
The quality factor and the resonance frequency will remain constant.
solution:
The bandwidth of resonance circuit will increase.

As we know that Bandwidth =R/L.

Bandwidth ∝R.

So, bandwidth will increase.

subject: Physics
q: In the figure shown, a circuit contains two identical resistors with resistance R=5 Ω and an inductance with L=2 mH. An ideal battery of 15V is connected in the circuit. What will be the current through the battery long after the switch is closed?
question: In the figure shown, a circuit contains two identical resistors with resistance R=5 Ω and an inductance with L=2 mH. An ideal battery of 15V is connected in the circuit. What will be the current through the battery long after the switch is closed?
solution:
After a long time, when the growth of current in the circuit has stopped, the inductor will work as a conductor. Therefore the effective resistance of the circuit will be, 

R=52=2.5 Ω

Hence, the current through the circuit will be,

I=VR=152.5=6A

q: A power transmission line feeds input power at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2300</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> to a step down transformer with its primary windings having <math><mn>4000</mn></math> turns. If the current in the primary of the transformer is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and its efficiency is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>90</mn><mo> </mo><mi>%</mi></math>, the output current would be:
question: A power transmission line feeds input power at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2300</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> to a step down transformer with its primary windings having <math><mn>4000</mn></math> turns. The output power is delivered at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>230</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> by the transformer. If the current in the primary of the transformer is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and its efficiency is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>90</mn><mo> </mo><mi>%</mi></math>, the output current would be:
solution:
Efficiency n=90%

⇒0.9×power in=Power out

0.9×2300×5=230×Iout

Iout=45 A

q: Magnetic materials used for making permanent magnets P and magnets in a transformer T have different properties of the following, which property best matches for the type of magnet required?
question: Magnetic materials used for making permanent magnets P and magnets in a transformer T have different properties of the following, which property best matches for the type of magnet required?
solution: As per the properties of Magnetic substances, materials favorable for permanent magnets should have high retentivity, large coercivity and high permeability.

subject: Physics
q: An alternating voltage source <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>260</mn><mi>sin</mi><mo>(</mo><mn>628</mn><mi>t</mi><mo>)</mo></math> is connected across a pure inductor of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mH</mi></math>. Inductive reactance in the circuit is:
question: An alternating voltage source <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>260</mn><mi>sin</mi><mo>(</mo><mn>628</mn><mi>t</mi><mo>)</mo></math> is connected across a pure inductor of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mH</mi></math>. Inductive reactance in the circuit is:
solution:
Comparing the given equation of AC voltage with standard expression, V=V0sinωt, we get ω=628 rad s-1.

Inductive reactance is given by, XL=ωL.

=628×5×10-3

=3.14 Ω

subject: Physics
q: An alternating emf $\mathrm{E}=110 \sqrt{2} \sin 100 \mathrm{t}$ volt is applied to a capacitor of $2 \mu \mathrm{F}$, the rms value of current in the circuit is _____$\mathrm{mA}$,
question: An alternating emf $\mathrm{E}=110 \sqrt{2} \sin 100 \mathrm{t}$ volt is applied to a capacitor of $2 \mu \mathrm{F}$, the rms value of current in the circuit is _____$\mathrm{mA}$,
solution: $\begin{aligned} C & =2 \mu f ; \quad E=110 \sqrt{2} \sin (100 \mathrm{t}) \\ \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{C}} & =\frac{1}{\omega \mathrm{c}}=\frac{1}{100 \times 2 \times 10^{-6}} \\ = & \frac{10000}{2}=5000 \Omega \\ \mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{o}} & =\frac{110 \sqrt{2}}{5000} \\ \mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{rms}} & =\frac{110 \sqrt{2}}{5000 \sqrt{2}} \\ & =\frac{110}{5} \mathrm{~mA} \\ & =22 \mathrm{~mA}\end{aligned}$

q: A series LCR circuit consists of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>80</mn><mo> </mo><mo>Ω</mo></math>. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>L</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mo>Ω</mo></math>, and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>C</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mo>Ω</mo></math>. The input voltage is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2500</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cos</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>100</mn><mi>π</mi><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The amplitude of current, in the circuit, is _____  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>.
question: A series LCR circuit consists of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>80</mn><mo> </mo><mo>Ω</mo></math>. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>L</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mo>Ω</mo></math>, and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>C</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mo>Ω</mo></math>. The input voltage is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2500</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cos</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>100</mn><mi>π</mi><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The amplitude of current, in the circuit, is _____  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>.
solution:
Comparing input voltage with standard expression, V=V0cosωt, we get ω=100π.

Now, impedance Z=R2+XL-XC2

=802+100-402

=100Ω

Therefore, peak current

i0=V0Z=2500100 A=25 A

subject: Physics
q: A transformer operating at primary voltage <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mi>kV</mi></math> and secondary voltage <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>160</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> serves a load of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>80</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kW</mi></math>. Assuming the transformer to be ideal with purely resistive load and working on unity power factor, the loads in the primary and secondary circuit would be
question: A transformer operating at primary voltage <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mi>kV</mi></math> and secondary voltage <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>160</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> serves a load of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>80</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kW</mi></math>. Assuming the transformer to be ideal with purely resistive load and working on unity power factor, the loads in the primary and secondary circuit would be
solution:
Since the transformer is assumed to be purely resistive, the power of the transformer can be calculated with P=V2R. Therefore,

For the primary coil,

P=8×1032RP=80×103 W

⇒RP=800 Ω

For the secondary coil,

P=1602RS=80×103 W

⇒RS=0.32Ω

subject: Physics
q: A metal wire of linear mass density of $9.8 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{m}$ is stretched with a tension of $10 \mathrm{~kg}$-wt between two rigid supports 1 metre apart. The wire passes at its middle point between the poles of a permanent magnet, and it vibrates in resonance when carrying an alternating current of frequency $n$. The frequency $n$ of the alternating source is
question: A metal wire of linear mass density of $9.8 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{m}$ is stretched with a tension of $10 \mathrm{~kg}$-wt between two rigid supports 1 metre apart. The wire passes at its middle point between the poles of a permanent magnet, and it vibrates in resonance when carrying an alternating current of frequency $n$. The frequency $n$ of the alternating source is
solution:
$n=\frac{1}{2 l} \sqrt{\frac{T}{\mu}} ; \quad I=1 m$
$\mathrm{T}=10 \mathrm{Kg}$ wt. $=10 \times 10=100 \mathrm{~N}$
$\mu=9.8 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{m}=9.8 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m} \quad \mathrm{n}=50 \mathrm{hz}$

subject: Physics
q: Using a variable frequency a.c. voltage source the maximum current measured in the given LCR circuit is 50 mA for $V=5 \sin (100 t)$ The values of $L$ and $R$ are shown in the figure. The capacitance of the capacitor (C) used is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mu \mathrm{F}$.
question: Using a variable frequency a.c. voltage source the maximum current measured in the given LCR circuit is 50 mA for $V=5 \sin (100 t)$ The values of $L$ and $R$ are shown in the figure. The capacitance of the capacitor (C) used is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mu \mathrm{F}$.
solution:
From $V = 5\sin(100t)$, angular frequency $\omega = 100$ rad/s
At resonance in LCR circuit: $X_L = X_C$, so $\omega L = \frac{1}{\omega C}$
Maximum current occurs at resonance: $I_{max} = \frac{V_{rms}}{R}$
$50 \times 10^{-3} = \frac{5/\sqrt{2}}{100}$ (verified at resonance)
From resonance condition: $100 \times 2 = \frac{1}{100 \times C}$
$200 = \frac{1}{100C}$
$C = \frac{1}{20000} = 5 \times 10^{-5}$ F = 50 μF

q: A <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>750</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mfenced><mi>rms</mi></mfenced></math> source is connected to a resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi><mo>,</mo></math> an inductance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1803</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math> and a capacitance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></math> all in series. The time in which the resistance (heat capacity  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo>/</mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> ) will get heated by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>.</mo></math> (assume no loss of heat to the surroundings) is close to :

The AC circuit is shown below.

Here,R=100, XL=Lω=0.1803×750×2π=850Ω

The capacitive reactance Xc=1Cω=110-5×2π×750=21.23 Ω

Total impedance Z=R2+XL-XC2

=1002+850-21.232=834.77≈835

The heat loss H=irms2Rt=ms∆t

20835×20835×100t=2×10

t=348.61 sec

subject: Physics
q: An oscillating <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>LC</mi><mo> </mo></math>circuit consists of a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>75</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mH</mi></math> inductor and a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mo>µ</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></math> capacitor. If the maximum charge to the capacitor is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>7</mn><mo> </mo><mo>µ</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. The maximum current in the circuit will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>_______</mi><mo> </mo><mi>mA</mi></math>.
question: An oscillating <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>LC</mi><mo> </mo></math>circuit consists of a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>75</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mH</mi></math> inductor and a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mo>µ</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></math> capacitor. If the maximum charge to the capacitor is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>7</mn><mo> </mo><mo>µ</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. The maximum current in the circuit will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>_______</mi><mo> </mo><mi>mA</mi></math>.
solution:
When the capacitor is fully discharged, the current is maximum and all the energy resides in the inductor. Hence, the energy of the capacitor is equal to the energy of the inductor.

Li2m2=Q2m2C   ...(i)

 where im is the maximum current and Qm is the maximum charge.

The given values are 

Qm=2.7×10-6 CL=75×10-3 HC=1.2×10-6 F

The maximum current is found from equation (i)

im=1LCQm⇒im=2.7×10-675×10-3×1.2×10-6=9 mA

subject: Physics
q: A series LCR circuit is subjected to an ac signal of $200 \mathrm{~V}, 50 \mathrm{~Hz}$. If the voltage across the inductor $(\mathrm{L}=10 \mathrm{mH})$ is $31.4 \mathrm{~V}$, then the current in this circuit is _____ .
question: A series LCR circuit is subjected to an ac signal of $200 \mathrm{~V}, 50 \mathrm{~Hz}$. If the voltage across the inductor $(\mathrm{L}=10 \mathrm{mH})$ is $31.4 \mathrm{~V}$, then the current in this circuit is _____ .
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { Voltage across inductor } \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{L}}=\mathrm{IX}_{\mathrm{L}} \\ & 31.4=\mathrm{I}[\mathrm{L} \omega] \\ & 31.4=\mathrm{I}[\mathrm{L}(2 \pi \mathrm{f})] \\ & 31.4=\mathrm{I}\left[10 \times 10^{-3}(2 \times 3.14) \times 50\right. \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{I}=10 \mathrm{~A}\end{aligned}$

question:

question: Find the peak current and resonant frequency of the following circuit (as shown in figure).
solution:
as given Z=XL-XC2+R2

XL=ωL=100×100×10-3=10 Ω

XC=1ωC=1100×100×10-6=100 Ω

Z=10-1002+R2=902+1202=30×5=150 Ω

ipeak =Vpeakz=30150=15 amp=0.2 amp

& For resonant frequency

& f=12πLC⇒12π100×10-3×100×10-6

=100102π=100π2π=50 Hz

as 10≈π

subject: Physics
q: Three identical resistors with resistance R=12 Ω and two identical inductors with sell inductance L=5 mH are connected to an ideal battery with emf of 12 V as shown in figure. The current through the battery long after the switch has been closed will be________A.
question: Three identical resistors with resistance R=12 Ω and two identical inductors with sell inductance L=5 mH are connected to an ideal battery with emf of 12 V as shown in figure. The current through the battery long after the switch has been closed will be________A.
solution:
We know that after long time an inductor offers zero resistance i.e., in steady state condition.

Here all three identical resistors are in parallel, so equivalent resistance is, Req=123=4 Ω

Now, current through the battery is I=124=3 A

subject: Physics
q: The equation of current in a purely inductive circuit is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>49</mn><mi>π</mi><mi>t</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></mrow></mfenced></math>. If the inductance is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mi>mH</mi></math> then the equation for the voltage across the inductor, will be
question: The equation of current in a purely inductive circuit is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>49</mn><mi>π</mi><mi>t</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></mrow></mfenced></math>. If the inductance is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mi>mH</mi></math> then the equation for the voltage across the inductor, will be
solution:
In inductive circuit, the voltage leads the current by 90°. Therefore,

ϕ0V-ϕ0I=90°⇒ϕ0V=60°

From the given equation 5sin49πt-30°, angular frequency ω=49π rad s-1

The amplitude of the voltage across the inductor will be,

V0=i0XL

⇒V0=i0ωL

⇒V0=549π30×10-3=23.1 

⇒V=23.1sin49πt+60°

subject: Physics
q: A capacitor of reactance $4 \sqrt{3} \Omega$ and a resistor of resistance $4 \Omega$ are connected in series with an ac source of peak value $8 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{~V}$. The power dissipation in the circuit is ________ W.
question: A capacitor of reactance $4 \sqrt{3} \Omega$ and a resistor of resistance $4 \Omega$ are connected in series with an ac source of peak value $8 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{~V}$. The power dissipation in the circuit is ________ W.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{Z}=\sqrt{\mathrm{R}^2+\mathrm{X}^2 \mathrm{~L}} \\ & \mathrm{Z}=\sqrt{4^2+(4 \sqrt{3})^2}=8 \Omega \\ & \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{rms}}=\frac{\mathrm{V}}{\sqrt{2}}=\frac{8 \sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{2}}=(8 \mathrm{~V}) \\ & \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{rms}}=\frac{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{rms}}}{\mathrm{Z}}=\frac{8}{8}=1 \mathrm{~A}\end{aligned}$ Power dissipated $=\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{rms}}^2 \times \mathrm{R}=1 \times 4=(4 \mathrm{~W})$

subject: Physics
q: In the given circuit the AC source has ω=100 rad s-1. Considering the inductor and capacitor to be ideal, what will be the current I flowing through the circuit?
question: In the given circuit the AC source has ω=100 rad s-1. Considering the inductor and capacitor to be ideal, what will be the current I flowing through the circuit?
solution:
ZC=1ωC2+R2

=1100×100×10-62+1002

=1002+1002

=1002 Ω

ZL=(ωL)2+R2=(100×0.5)2+502=502 Ω

iL=200ZL=200502=42=22 A

iC=200ZC=2001002=2 A

cosϕ1=1001002=12, ϕ1=45°

cosϕ2=50502=12, ϕ2=45°

I=IC2+IL2;  I=8+2;  I=10=3.16 A

This question is bonus as the correct answer was not available for students to mark as option in original paper by NTA.
